article.title: Apache Graduates Gluten and Polaris to Top-Level Projects, Bolstering the Open Data Lakehouse Stack
article.summary: The Apache Software Foundation promotes two data infrastructure projects from incubation, giving Spark a native-speed accelerator and Iceberg its first vendor-neutral catalog standard.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

The Apache Software Foundation (ASF) has graduated two open-source data infrastructure projects -- Apache Gluten and Apache Polaris -- from incubation to Top-Level Project (TLP) status, as [announced on March 5](https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/03/05/3250238/17401/en/The-Apache-Software-Foundation-Graduates-Two-Open-Source-Projects-from-Incubator.html). The promotions add a native-code Spark accelerator and a vendor-neutral Iceberg catalog to the ASF's portfolio, filling two gaps that the modern data lakehouse stack has struggled with: JVM performance ceilings in Spark and proprietary lock-in at the catalog layer of Apache Iceberg.

## Apache Gluten: Moving Spark Off the JVM Hot Path

Apache Gluten is a middle-layer plugin that offloads compute-intensive SQL and DataFrame operations from Apache Spark's JVM runtime to high-performance native engines such as Meta's Velox and ClickHouse. Originally created by Intel and Kyligence in early 2022, the project entered the Apache Incubator and has since attracted contributors from across the data engineering ecosystem.

The performance gains are substantial. Benchmarks on TPC-H-like workloads have shown an overall speedup of 3.3x, with individual queries running up to 23x faster, [according to Intel](https://community.intel.com/t5/Blogs/Tech-Innovation/Cloud/Increase-Spark-Performance-with-Intel-CPUs-and-Gluten/post/1637160). The architecture is designed to be extensible, with future integration planned for additional ASF technologies including Apache DataFusion.

"Graduating to an Apache Top-Level Project represents an important step forward for Gluten," said Weiting Chen, the project's PMC Chair, in the [ASF announcement](https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/03/05/3250238/17401/en/The-Apache-Software-Foundation-Graduates-Two-Open-Source-Projects-from-Incubator.html). "We are committed to building a solution that makes Spark faster and more efficient, enabling better real-time analytics and lower costs for big data workloads."

## Apache Polaris: An Open Catalog Standard for Iceberg

Apache Polaris is a cloud-native catalog for Apache Iceberg that implements the Iceberg REST API specification, enabling any compliant engine to discover and manage Iceberg tables through a single, vendor-neutral service. Snowflake originally created and [announced Polaris at its June 2024 summit](https://venturebeat.com/data-infrastructure/snowflake-unveils-polaris-a-vendor-neutral-open-catalog-implementation-of-apache-iceberg), then open-sourced it under the Apache 2.0 license in July 2024. The project entered the Apache Incubator in August 2024.

Polaris addresses a structural gap in the Iceberg ecosystem. While Iceberg standardized the table format, the catalog layer -- which manages metadata, access control, and multi-engine coordination -- remained proprietary. Polaris provides a community-driven, open alternative that supports cross-engine interoperability across Apache Doris, Apache Flink, Apache Spark, Dremio OSS, StarRocks, and Trino.

During its roughly 18 months of incubation, the project shipped six releases, attracted approximately 100 contributors from multiple organizations, and closed more than 2,800 pull requests. The graduation vote received unanimous support from binding voters before ASF Board approval, as detailed in the [February 19 announcement](https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/02/19/3240735/0/en/apache-polaris-graduates-to-top-level-apache-project.html).

"This promotion highlights the maturity of the Polaris community and its technology," said Jean-Baptiste Onofre, the project's PMC Chair. "Our community is excited to continue providing new levels of choice, flexibility, and control over data."

## What This Means for the Data Lakehouse Ecosystem

The twin graduations arrive as the data lakehouse architecture -- which combines data lake storage economics with data warehouse query capabilities -- is becoming the default pattern for enterprise analytics and AI workloads. Apache Iceberg has emerged as the dominant open table format, but organizations adopting it have faced two recurring friction points: Spark's JVM overhead on compute-intensive queries, and catalog-layer lock-in that ties data to specific vendors.

Gluten tackles the first problem by letting Spark users stay on the Spark API while routing execution through native code paths. Polaris tackles the second by ensuring that the catalog managing Iceberg metadata is itself open, governed by the Apache Way, and not controlled by any single vendor.

## What We Don't Know

Neither project has published detailed adoption figures for production deployments. Gluten's benchmarks, while impressive, were measured on TPC-H and TPC-DS workloads that may not reflect all real-world query patterns. Polaris faces competition from other Iceberg catalog implementations, including Nessie and proprietary offerings from cloud providers, and it remains to be seen whether its ASF governance model will prove sufficient to maintain vendor neutrality as commercial interests deepen.

The ASF's incubation-to-TLP track record is generally strong, but graduating is only the beginning. Both projects will need sustained contributor engagement and production hardening to deliver on their promise of an open, high-performance data lakehouse stack.
